answer: Argon2id is used with a memory cost of 64MB, 3 iterations, and a parallelism factor of 4.
Refresh tokens expire in 7 days and rotate on every use.
The Notification Service runs on port 9200.
The database is currently running RDS PostgreSQL 15.4, having been upgraded from 14.9.
API Gateway throttling limits are 5000 req/s burst and 2000 req/s steady state.
Argon2id was chosen for its resistance to side-channel attacks compared to bcrypt.
Drizzle ORM is used because it has no runtime dependencies and produces SQL-like TypeScript.
They verify RS256-signed JWTs using the JWKS endpoint located at /.well-known/jwks.json.
ALB-level rate limiting is used to protect against DDoS attacks before requests reach the service containers.
The chain involves the API Gateway (where a custom authorizer Lambda validates the JWT and injects claims) and the Portfolio Service itself, which also verifies the token via the JWKS endpoint.

If the JWKS endpoint goes down, services relying on it (Portfolio, Notification, API Gateway) will fail to verify tokens once their LRU cache expires (5-minute TTL). There is no mentioned grace period beyond the cache TTL.
Real-time portfolio alerting depends on the API Gateway WebSocket API; it is currently on the Q3 roadmap.
Yes, it was fixed by switching all expiry calculations to use Unix timestamps instead of Date objects.
Register the enterprise client via POST /api/v1/notifications/webhooks/register; the system handles delivery with exponential backoff retries over 5 attempts and 24 hours.
With 20 connections per instance and 5 instances, the maximum is 100 simultaneous database connections.
The connection pool is limited to 20 per instance, while the Celery worker memory issue is an independent unbounded growth problem currently under investigation during large recalculations.
No, Fargate Spot is only for non-critical batch workloads; Auth Service requires always-on availability to meet zero-downtime deployment requirements.
The requirement for stateless horizontal scaling prohibits in-memory session caches.
The 30-day retention provides the data source for meeting the RPO (Recovery Point Objective) of 1 hour and RTO (Recovery Time Objective) of 4 hours during disaster recovery.
The upgrade required adding '?sslmode=verify-full&sslrootcert=global-bundle.pem' to the connection string.
The columns are id, account_id, security_id, acquisition_date, cost_basis, quantity, adjusted, and created_at.
When enabled, MFA is required during the login flow; setup involves a QR code URI and the generation of recovery codes.
10 single-use recovery codes are generated; they are permanently consumed upon use.
The policy is exponential backoff with 5 attempts over 24 hours.

At rest: PII is encrypted via AES-256-GCM with per-tenant keys, and secrets are in AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation. In transit: RS256 JWTs, mTLS via App Mesh/Envoy, and RDS connections use verify-full SSL mode.

Request hits API Gateway -> Custom Authorizer validates initial token -> Auth Service verifies credentials -> Auth Service checks MFA status -> User provides TOTP/Recovery Code -> Auth Service issues new RS256 JWT and refresh token.
It must use RS256 JWT validation via JWKS, implement mTLS via App Mesh, use structured JSON logging, support blue-green deployments, and undergo Claude Code review.
The ALB rate limit (1000 req/min) will drop 500 requests; the remaining 1000 pass to the Auth Service containers, provided they don't exceed API Gateway's 2000 req/s steady-state limit.
The Auth Service (for signing), and the Portfolio Service, Notification Service, and API Gateway Custom Authorizer (for verification via JWKS).
The pipeline includes linting, type-checking, testing, building the image, pushing to ECR, and a blue-green deployment to ECS, with a mandatory Claude Code review before merging to main.
